author: Diode Dynamics
image_descriptions: Zeitronix wideband A/F gauge (round, shows 14.7 AFR)
content:
INTRO

So, you've got a couple mods on your car and you want to tune it. But how? By this point, you should have already purchased and installed a tool to change the signal coming from your MAF, known as a 'piggyback fuel tuner.' (I will be writing another thread on this in the next few days.)

Ok, so now you can change things, but how do you know how much to change? If you add 15% fuel, what happens to your A/F ratio?

The most simple way to find out is to take your car to a dyno, where they have equipment to measure this ratio, usually situated at your tailpipe.

But if you're in the mood for some major tuning, a few hours at a $70/hour dyno can get expensive! Almost makes you wish you could do it yourself!

That's where A/F measurement tools come in.


NARROWBAND VS WIDEBAND

First, we must understand the difference between 'narrowband' and 'wideband.' These terms refer to the signal coming from an oxygen (O2) sensor in the exhaust manifold. Most cars in current production use the cheaper 0v-1v narrowband O2 sensors in engine management. This is because the narrowband sensor measures the amount of oxygen and then sends anywhere from a 0 volt to 1 volt signal, which only tells the ECU if there is an extremely rich or lean signal, in which case it throws a CEL. This system is great for car manufacturers, because all they care about is if the car is working properly — not the A/F numbers in between.

A wideband signal, on the other hand, measures from rich, 0v, to lean, 5v. This broader range of possible electric current allows for a precise measurement of how much oxygen is in the air to be taken.

Contrary to popular belief, 2003+ Tiburons use wideband 0v-5v Titanium sensors in engine management, not narrowband. This means that for a 2003+ Tiburon, a narrowband A/F gauge will NOT work — you must use a wideband gauge.


GAUGES

You can constantly find A/F gauges on eBay. These are not the gauges that you want. These are almost always narrowband gauges. Narrowband gauges do just about nothing, because the narrowband signal is just that-narrow. It only measures 0v-1v, lean or rich. This allows for barely any measurement of an A/F ratio. It can sometimes be noticed when the car is at WOT, but other than that, it will just give you a pretty light show with the needle moving speratically. In addition to that, the narrowband sensors use a narrowband signal, 0v-1v, so they do not work at all on pre-03 Tiburons (with wideband sensors, as explained above).

This being said, you can probably guess that the only way to really get a good A/F measurement is with wideband.


SO WHAT DO I NEED FOR WIDEBAND MEASUREMENT?

To really accurately measure your A/F ratio, you need a wideband. This 'wideband' is actually just short for a wideband controller system. Basically, you will need: a wideband O2 sensor, capable of 0v-5v signals, a wideband controller, to 'read' and figure out what the sensor's data means and turn it into an A/F ratio, and a WIDEBAND A/F gauge to display the A/F number precisely. This can cost you anywhere from $150-600 for everything you need. There are many options.

To start, the Innovate Motorsports LC-1. This unit is basically a wideband O2 sensor with a controller at the end of the wire, with a plug to plug in a gauge. This is a convenient option for those wishing to pick a certain gauge or displaying tool. It also typically offers datalogging and a digital display known as a DB Gauge interface.
[IMAGE: Innovate LC-1 controller + gauge (images broken)]

Another option, AEM makes an interesting setup called the UEGO. Basically, it's a controller in the gauge, and comes with a wideband O2 sensor. This is a good option for those just wanting an A/F ratio, and not adding other things like boost, which some wideband controllers accommodate. It is not capable of datalogging, although it does have an output.
[IMAGE: AEM UEGO digital and analog display versions (images broken)]

Many Tiburon guys use the Zeitronix Zt-2 wideband controller. This unit allows for datalogging of the A/F signal with the included software and a laptop. Many also opt for buying a wideband gauge to display the number on-the-go in realtime. There are more than the circular gauges for doing this. There are LCD displays like the Zeitronix one.
[IMAGE: Zeitronix Zt-2 controller unit]
[IMAGE: Zeitronix round wideband A/F gauge showing 14.7]

Also, there are normal A/F gauges. When buying an A/F gauge, make SURE that it is WIDEBAND. A narrowband A/F gauge will not work to display a wideband signal. Many wideband controllers have their own plug-and-go wideband gauges, like the Zeitronix WB A/F gauge.
[IMAGE: LD02 display unit]
[IMAGE: te WIDEBAND O2 display]

When buying a controller, look for capabilities such as datalogging, an integrated fuse, and a low (0.1) AFR accuracy. This means that it will be accurate within .1 in the AFR output.

Once you have the controller, you need a gauge. You can easily tell if the gauge is wideband or not because it will almost always say 'WIDEBAND' on the gauge, because this is somewhat of a label of pride, showing people that this is actually an accurate wideband system and not an eBay narrowband. Of course, you can also use small LCD displays like the LD02 Display Unit, rather than using the more-conventional gauge display.

To install any wideband system, unless you have a pre-03, you will have to take it to a shop and add a bung (usually included in kit) to put your new wideband sensor in. Then it's just a bunch of wiring, which can sometimes be tricky. Once you have all of that done, you're ready to start accurately measuring (and even datalogging!) your A/F ratio.

Hope you enjoyed! I hope to add more on what A/F ratios mean at a later time. That's enough for now! Discuss!
extraction_notes: Full OP captured after 'See more' click. Some product images broken (old hosting) — described above. Pre-03 Tiburons have wideband sensors already (important note). 2003+ use 0v-5v titanium wideband sensors in stock engine management.
